walkthrough.title: Unfold the World Map
walkthrough.text: Let's get Twilight Struggle set up! Unfold the world map board and place it centrally on the table. The map shows six regions: Europe, the Middle East, Asia, Africa, Central America, and South America. Each region contains countries with stability numbers and connections to adjacent countries.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Tracks
walkthrough.text: Let's locate the important tracks on the board. The VP track runs from negative twenty to positive twenty. Positive favors the USA and negative favors the USSR. The DEFCON track runs from one to five. The Space Race track has boxes for each advancement. The Military Operations track resets each turn.

walkthrough.title: Set the Starting Markers
walkthrough.text: Place the VP marker on zero at the center of the VP track. Set the DEFCON marker on five, which means peace. Place the turn marker on turn one. Set both Space Race markers on the starting space. Set both Military Operations markers on zero. These tracks drive the game's tension.

walkthrough.title: Choose Sides
walkthrough.text: One player takes the USSR and the other takes the USA. The USSR player collects all red influence markers and the USA player collects all blue influence markers. The USSR player also receives the China Card face up. This special card provides four operations points, or five if all spent in Asia.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Influence
walkthrough.text: Both players place their starting influence markers on the board as printed. The USSR starts with influence in Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and Asia. The USA starts with influence in Western Europe, Canada, and key allies like Israel and Japan. Check the rulebook for exact placements.

walkthrough.title: Place Bonus Influence
walkthrough.text: After placing printed influence, each player gets bonus influence. The USSR places six additional influence markers anywhere in Eastern Europe. The USA places seven additional influence markers anywhere in Western Europe. This bonus lets you customize your defensive perimeter from the very start.

walkthrough.title: Separate the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Sort all event cards into three piles by their backs or period markings: Early War, Mid War, and Late War. The Early War cards are used from turn one. Mid War cards are shuffled in at turn four. Late War cards are added at turn eight. Set the Mid and Late War piles aside for now.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal Early War Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Early War deck thoroughly. Deal eight cards face down to each player. Look at your hand but keep it hidden from your opponent. These cards will drive all of your actions for the first turn. Get familiar with their event text and operations values.

walkthrough.title: Understand Card Affiliations
walkthrough.text: Before we start, let's understand card affiliations. Cards marked with a red star are USSR events. Cards with a blue star are USA events. Neutral cards can be played by either side. Here is the critical rule: if you play an opponent's card for operations, their event still triggers. This tension is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: Review Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Look through your hand for any scoring cards. There are six total, one for each region, shuffled into the deck. Scoring cards must be played before the end of the turn or you instantly lose. If you have one, plan when to play it. Never hold a scoring card past the end of a turn.

walkthrough.title: Understand Battleground Countries
walkthrough.text: Let's identify the battleground countries on the map. These are the countries with shaded stability numbers. Battleground countries are critical because coups there lower DEFCON, they score bonus VP during region scoring, and controlling all battlegrounds in a region achieves the powerful Control-level scoring.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Begin the Cold War
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. The USSR player will go first in every action round. Each turn begins with a Headline Phase where both players simultaneously select and reveal one card. Then alternating action rounds follow. Let's learn how turns work and begin the struggle for global influence.

summary.title: Ready to Begin the Cold War
bullets: USSR goes first every action round
Turn: Headline Phase → alternating Action Rounds
Let's learn the turn structure

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn has three phases. First, the Headline Phase where both players simultaneously play one card. Second, the Action Round phase where players alternate playing cards one at a time. Third, end-of-turn maintenance. Early War turns have six action rounds. Mid and Late War turns have seven.

walkthrough.title: The Headline Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of every turn, both players secretly choose one card from their hand and reveal them simultaneously. The card with the higher operations value resolves its event first, with the USSR breaking ties. Both events trigger regardless of card affiliation. This is your opening salvo for the turn.

walkthrough.title: Action Rounds: Playing a Card
walkthrough.text: During action rounds, the USSR always acts first. On your turn, you play one card from your hand. You have three choices for how to use it: play it for operations, play it for its event text, or discard it to the Space Race. Each choice has very different strategic implications.

walkthrough.title: Operations: Place Influence
walkthrough.text: When using a card for operations, you can place influence markers. Each operations point lets you place one influence marker in a country where you already have influence or that is adjacent to one. If the target country is controlled by your opponent, it costs two operations points per marker instead of one.

walkthrough.title: Operations: Coup
walkthrough.text: A coup is an aggressive operation. Roll one die and add the card's operations value. If the total exceeds double the country's stability number, the coup succeeds. Remove enemy influence first, then add yours with any remainder. Couping a battleground country lowers DEFCON by one. Coups also add to your Military Operations track.

walkthrough.title: Operations: Realignment
walkthrough.text: Realignment is a less aggressive operation that removes enemy influence without adding your own. Both players roll a die for the targeted country, with bonuses for having more influence and adjacent controlled countries. The difference between rolls removes influence from the loser. You do not need presence in the target country.

walkthrough.title: Playing for the Event
walkthrough.text: Instead of operations, you can play a card purely for its event text. Resolve the event as written. Some events are one-time effects and some are permanent. If the card has an asterisk, it is removed from the game after the event triggers. Otherwise, it goes to the discard pile and will return.

walkthrough.title: The Critical Opponent Event Rule
walkthrough.text: This is the most important rule in the game. If you play a card with your opponent's event for operations, their event still triggers. You get your operations, but the event happens too. The timing depends on the card. This forced dilemma of helping your opponent while helping yourself is the core tension of Twilight Struggle.

walkthrough.title: The Space Race
walkthrough.text: The Space Race lets you discard a card without triggering its event. This is your safety valve for dangerous opponent cards. The card must meet a minimum operations value shown on the next Space Race box. Roll a die to advance. You are limited to one Space Race attempt per turn unless a track bonus says otherwise.

walkthrough.title: DEFCON: The Nuclear Threat
walkthrough.text: DEFCON starts at five and degrades when battleground countries are couped or certain events fire. DEFCON improves by one at the start of each turn. If DEFCON ever reaches one, the phasing player, meaning the player whose action caused it, instantly loses the game. This is thermonuclear war and it ends everything.

walkthrough.title: DEFCON Restrictions
walkthrough.text: As DEFCON drops, regions become restricted for coups and realignment. At DEFCON four, no coups on battlegrounds in Europe. At DEFCON three, no coups or realignment in Europe or Asia. At DEFCON two, Europe, Asia, and the Middle East are all locked. These restrictions dramatically shape your strategic options.

walkthrough.title: Controlling Countries
walkthrough.text: You control a country when your influence exceeds your opponent's influence by at least the country's stability number. Controlling countries is how you score during region scoring. Track which countries you control in contested regions because scoring cards can appear at any time.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: When a scoring card is played, the matching region is evaluated. Three levels exist: Presence means you control at least one country. Domination means you control more countries and more battlegrounds plus at least one non-battleground. Control means you control all battleground countries and more total countries. VP is awarded based on the level achieved.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Europe: Instant Win
walkthrough.text: Europe scoring has a special rule. If either player achieves Control of Europe when the Europe Scoring card is played, that player wins immediately regardless of the VP track. This makes Europe the most critical region in the game. Never leave Europe uncontested.

walkthrough.title: Military Operations
walkthrough.text: Each turn, both players must reach Military Operations equal to the current DEFCON level or lose VP. Coups are the primary way to gain Military Operations. If DEFCON is at four and your Military Operations are at two, you lose two VP at end of turn. This creates pressure to coup early each turn.

walkthrough.title: The China Card
walkthrough.text: The China Card provides four operations points, or five if all points are spent in Asia. After playing it, flip it face down and pass it to your opponent. They can use it next turn. The China Card cannot be played during the Headline Phase and does not count as part of your hand limit.

walkthrough.title: End-of-Turn Maintenance
walkthrough.text: At the end of each turn, check Military Operations penalties, flip the China Card face up if it was played, reset Military Operations to zero, advance the turn marker, deal cards so each player has eight, and on specific turns shuffle in the Mid War or Late War decks. Players keep unplayed cards from the previous turn.

walkthrough.title: Instant Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game can end instantly in several ways. Reaching plus twenty VP means USA wins. Reaching negative twenty means USSR wins. Controlling Europe during scoring wins immediately. DEFCON reaching one means the phasing player loses. Holding a scoring card at end of turn means instant loss.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring at Turn Ten
walkthrough.text: If no instant victory occurs by the end of turn ten, all six regions are scored. The player holding the China Card gains one VP. The player with the higher VP total wins. VP above zero favors the USA and VP below zero favors the USSR. Most games end before turn ten through VP accumulation.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Cold War
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Twilight Struggle! We will walk through the first turn of a two-player game. One player controls the USSR and the other controls the USA. The board is set up with starting influence placed. Both players have eight Early War cards. Let's begin with the Headline Phase.

walkthrough.title: The Headline Phase
walkthrough.text: Both players secretly choose a headline card. The USSR plays Defectors, a three operations USA event. The USA plays NATO, a four operations USA event. NATO has higher ops, so it resolves first. NATO gives the USA defensive power in Europe. Then Defectors resolves, canceling the USA event. The USSR player just neutralized NATO in the headline.

walkthrough.title: Action Round One: USSR Coups
walkthrough.text: The USSR goes first and plays Arab-Israeli War for operations, using the three ops to attempt a coup in Iran. The die roll plus three operations gives a total of seven, which exceeds double Iran's stability of two. The coup succeeds, removing USA influence and adding USSR influence. DEFCON drops from five to four because Iran is a battleground.

walkthrough.title: Action Round One: USA Responds
walkthrough.text: The USA needs to stabilize. They play a three operations card to place influence in key countries. They add influence to Pakistan, Thailand, and South Korea, establishing a defensive perimeter in Asia. The USA is playing reactively while the USSR pushes aggressively. This is the typical Early War dynamic.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Triggering DEFCON One
walkthrough.text: In action round three, the USSR considers couping West Germany, a battleground country. But DEFCON is already at three, and DEFCON restrictions prohibit coups on European battleground countries at DEFCON four or below. If the USSR somehow triggered DEFCON one, they would instantly lose. Always check DEFCON before couping.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check DEFCON
walkthrough.text: Before every coup, look at the DEFCON track. If couping a battleground will drop DEFCON to one, do not do it. You will lose instantly. Also check DEFCON restrictions to see which regions are locked. At DEFCON three, Europe and Asia are off-limits for coups and realignment. Plan your coups for regions that are still open.

walkthrough.title: Playing an Opponent's Card
walkthrough.text: The USA holds De-Stalinization, a USSR event with four operations. The USA needs those four ops but playing the card triggers the USSR event. The USA decides to play it for operations and places influence in four countries. Then the USSR event resolves, letting the USSR rearrange their influence markers. Both sides benefit, but it was the best option available.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Managing Opponent Events
walkthrough.text: You will always have opponent event cards in your hand. You have three options: play them for ops and accept the event, Space Race them to avoid the event, or hold them and play them last when their impact is minimized. The Space Race is your best tool for truly dangerous cards. Plan your card order each turn with this in mind.

walkthrough.title: Using the Space Race
walkthrough.text: The USSR holds CIA Created, a dangerous USA event that reveals the USSR hand. Rather than triggering it for ops, the USSR discards it to the Space Race. The card is gone and the event does not fire. The USSR rolls the die and advances on the Space Race track, gaining a bonus. This is the Space Race at its best.

walkthrough.title: The Scoring Card Dilemma
walkthrough.text: The USA holds the Europe Scoring card. Europe is contested but the USA has slight Domination. The USA could play it now for VP or hold it to try to strengthen their position first. But holding scoring cards is risky because forgetting to play it before the turn ends means instant loss.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Holding Scoring Cards Too Long
walkthrough.text: The USA decides to hold Europe Scoring for one more action round. Then the USSR plays a card that flips a key European country. Now the scoring would give the USA less VP. Worse, the USA almost forgot to play it before the turn ended. Holding scoring cards too long risks both worse scoring and the instant-loss penalty.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play Scoring Cards Promptly
walkthrough.text: When you draw a scoring card, plan when to play it immediately. If the region currently favors you, play it early before your opponent can disrupt your position. If the region is unfavorable, you must still play it before the turn ends. Never let a scoring card be the last card you have to remember.

walkthrough.title: Military Operations Pressure
walkthrough.text: At the end of the turn, both players check Military Operations. DEFCON is at three, so both need at least three Military Operations. The USSR achieved three through their early coup. The USA only has two and loses one VP for the shortfall. The USA should have couped a non-battleground country earlier to build Mil Ops.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Military Operations
walkthrough.text: New USA players often focus entirely on influence placement and forget about Military Operations. Every turn, check your Mil Ops against the DEFCON level. If you are short, plan a coup early in the turn, ideally in a non-battleground country where the DEFCON penalty does not apply.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Coup Early for Military Operations
walkthrough.text: Make your first or second action round each turn a coup if you need Military Operations. Couping non-battleground countries does not lower DEFCON but still counts toward your Mil Ops requirement. This also puts offensive pressure on your opponent. Do not wait until the last action round to realize you are short.

walkthrough.title: End of Turn One
walkthrough.text: Turn one concludes. DEFCON improves by one at the start of turn two. Both players keep unplayed cards and are dealt new cards to bring their hands back to eight. The Mid War deck will be shuffled in at turn four, introducing more powerful events and new scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Early War Summary
walkthrough.text: The first three turns are the Early War. The USSR has stronger events and should press advantages aggressively. The USA plays defense, establishes global presence, and waits for the Mid and Late War when their events become dominant. Both players are jockeying for position across all six regions.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through your first turn of Twilight Struggle. You learned the Headline Phase, how to coup and place influence, the dangers of DEFCON, how to manage opponent events with the Space Race, and why scoring cards demand immediate attention.

walkthrough.title: Ready for Global Domination
walkthrough.text:
Before you continue your game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on card management, region priorities, and DEFCON manipulation.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all mechanics, terms, and key cards.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Ready for Global Domination

Reference the Appendix → mechanics, terms, key cards
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Score Objectives, Not Influence
walkthrough.text: Influence on the board does not score VP by itself. Only scoring cards convert board position into VP. Focus your operations on regions where scoring cards are likely to appear soon. Spreading influence everywhere is less effective than dominating the regions that matter most right now.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Card Order
walkthrough.text: At the start of each turn, look at your entire hand and plan which card to play in which action round. Identify opponent events, scoring cards, and your strongest plays. Play dangerous opponent events early or Space Race them. Play scoring cards when the region favors you. Save your best cards for when they have maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: Coup Early for Military Operations
walkthrough.text: Your first or second action round should usually be a coup. This builds Military Operations toward the DEFCON requirement and puts offensive pressure on your opponent. Coup non-battleground countries in open regions to avoid dropping DEFCON into dangerous territory.

walkthrough.title: Use the Space Race Wisely
walkthrough.text: The Space Race is your escape valve for the most dangerous opponent events. You can only Space Race once per turn, so choose carefully. The card with the worst event should be your Space Race candidate. Do not waste the Space Race on a card you could safely play for operations.

walkthrough.title: Never Neglect Europe
walkthrough.text: Europe is worth the most VP during scoring and can trigger an instant win through Control. Even if you are focused on other regions, always maintain enough presence in Europe to prevent your opponent from achieving Control. Losing Europe can end the game immediately.

walkthrough.title: Control Battleground Countries
walkthrough.text: Battleground countries are worth bonus VP during scoring and are required for Domination and Control level scoring. Prioritize placing influence in battleground countries over non-battlegrounds. A region with all battlegrounds controlled and one non-battleground is worth much more than scattered non-battleground presence.

walkthrough.title: Learn the Key Events
walkthrough.text: Twilight Struggle has over one hundred event cards. Learning which events exist in each war period lets you anticipate threats and plan defenses. Key early war events include De-Stalinization, Marshall Plan, and Korean War. Knowing what is coming helps you position defensively before the threat arrives.

walkthrough.title: Manage DEFCON as a Weapon
walkthrough.text: DEFCON restrictions lock regions as DEFCON drops. You can use this strategically. Dropping DEFCON through a battleground coup in the Middle East locks Europe from coups at DEFCON four. If you are strong in Europe, this protects your position. Use DEFCON restrictions to seal regions where you have an advantage.

summary.title: Score Objectives, Not Influence
bullets: Influence doesn't score VP — scoring cards do
Focus ops on regions where scoring is likely

summary.title: Plan Your Card Order
bullets: Plan all 6-7 ARs at turn start
Dangerous opponent events first or to Space Race
Save best cards for max impact

summary.title: Coup Early for Military Operations
bullets: AR1 or AR2 → coup non-battleground in open region
Builds Mil Ops + offensive pressure

summary.title: Use the Space Race Wisely
bullets: 1/turn — save for worst opponent event
Don't waste on cards you could safely play for ops

summary.title: Never Neglect Europe
bullets: Highest VP value + instant win through Control
Always maintain enough presence to prevent opponent Control

summary.title: Control Battleground Countries

Prioritize battlegrounds over non-battlegrounds

summary.title: Learn the Key Events
bullets: 100+ cards — learn each war period's threats
Anticipate and position defensively before threats arrive

summary.title: Manage DEFCON as a Weapon
bullets: Lower DEFCON locks regions from coups/realignment
Seal regions where you have advantage

walkthrough.title: The DEFCON Trap
walkthrough.text: An advanced tactic is engineering situations where your opponent is forced to trigger DEFCON one on their own action. If DEFCON is at two and you play an event that forces your opponent to coup a battleground, they lose because they are the phasing player. This requires precise card knowledge and timing.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Track which scoring cards and key events have been played. If five of six scoring cards have appeared and only South America remains, you know exactly where to invest. Similarly, if a dangerous event like Lone Gunman has not yet appeared, plan for it. Card counting transforms uncertain threats into manageable risks.

walkthrough.title: Headline Warfare
walkthrough.text: The Headline Phase is not random. Choose headlines that set up your entire turn. A high-ops headline lets you resolve first, potentially disrupting your opponent's plan before their headline even fires. Headlining scoring cards can catch an opponent off-guard before they have a chance to realign.

walkthrough.title: Overprotection and Stability
walkthrough.text: In key countries, place more influence than the minimum needed for control. This creates a buffer that survives one or two realignment attempts or coup results. A country with your influence exceeding control by two is much harder to flip than one at exact control threshold.

walkthrough.title: Region Tempo Shifting
walkthrough.text: Early War focuses on Europe and the Middle East. Mid War opens Africa and Central America. Late War often decides the game in marginal regions. Experienced players shift focus to match the current card pool. When Mid War cards enter, pivot attention to the newly scoreable regions before your opponent does.

walkthrough.title: Access Through Influence Chains
walkthrough.text: Influence placement requires adjacency. Build chains of influence through connected countries to reach deep into enemy territory. For example, placing influence through Pakistan to India to Thailand creates an access route across Asia. Breaking an opponent's chain by flipping a single country can cut off their expansion.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Card Timing Mastery
walkthrough.text: If you hold a scoring card for a region you dominate, consider the optimal moment to play it. Playing it immediately after strengthening the region with influence locks in VP before your opponent can respond. Conversely, if the region is weak, play it on the last action round to minimize time your opponent has to prepare.

walkthrough.title: Playing as USSR vs USA
walkthrough.text: Each side has a distinct arc. The USSR should press hard in the Early War, spreading influence aggressively and exploiting strong Early War events. The USA should play defense, establish global Presence, and survive until Mid and Late War when their events become dominant. Understanding this asymmetric tempo is the foundation of high-level play.

summary.title: The DEFCON Trap
bullets: Force opponent to trigger DEFCON 1 on their action
Requires precise card knowledge and DEFCON 2 timing

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: Track which scoring cards and key events have appeared
5 of 6 scoring cards played → know where to invest

summary.title: Headline Warfare
bullets: High-ops headline resolves first → disrupt opponent's plan
Headline scoring cards to catch opponent off-guard

summary.title: Overprotection and Stability
bullets: Place extra influence beyond control minimum
Buffer survives realignment/coup attempts

summary.title: Region Tempo Shifting
bullets: Early: Europe/Middle East; Mid: Africa/C. America; Late: margins
Pivot focus to match current card pool

summary.title: Access Through Influence Chains
bullets: Build adjacency chains to reach deep into enemy territory
Flip 1 country in a chain → cut off expansion

summary.title: Scoring Card Timing Mastery
bullets: Strong region → play after strengthening (lock VP)
Weak region → play last AR (minimize opponent prep time)

summary.title: Playing as USSR vs USA
bullets: USSR: aggressive Early War, spread influence fast
USA: defend, establish Presence, thrive in Mid/Late War
Asymmetric tempo = foundation of high-level play

entries.definition: A colored token (red for USSR, blue for USA) placed in countries to represent political influence. The foundation of all board control.
A track running from negative twenty (USSR advantage) to positive twenty (USA advantage). Reaching either extreme triggers an instant win.
A track from one to five representing nuclear tension. Five is peace. One is thermonuclear war and instant loss for the phasing player.
A track where players advance by discarding cards. Provides VP bonuses for reaching milestones first and serves as a dump for dangerous opponent events.
A track resetting each turn. Must reach the current DEFCON level by turn end or lose VP equal to the shortfall. Primarily advanced through coups.
A special card providing four operations points (five if all spent in Asia). After use, passed face-down to opponent. Cannot be headlined.
A number printed on each country indicating how difficult it is to control or coup. Control requires influence exceeding the opponent by this number. Coups must exceed double this value.
A country with a shaded stability number. Coups here lower DEFCON. Worth bonus VP during scoring. Required for Control-level region scoring.
A player controls a country when their influence exceeds their opponent's by at least the stability number. In region scoring, Control means all battlegrounds plus more total countries.
The lowest level of region scoring. Achieved by controlling at least one country in the region. Awards the smallest VP amount.
The middle level of region scoring. Requires controlling more countries, more battlegrounds, and at least one non-battleground in the region.
The highest level of region scoring. Requires controlling all battleground countries and more total countries in the region. Awards the highest VP.
One of six cards (one per region) that triggers VP scoring when played. Must be played before the turn ends or the holder instantly loses.
An event card in the starting deck, used from turn one. These cards define the opening phase of the game and tend to favor the USSR.
An event card shuffled into the deck at the start of turn four. Introduces more powerful events and opens new strategic fronts.
An event card shuffled into the deck at the start of turn eight. Contains the most powerful events and often decides the endgame.
A card marked with a red star. Its event benefits the USSR. If the USA plays it for operations, the USSR event still triggers.
A card marked with a blue star. Its event benefits the USA. If the USSR plays it for operations, the USA event still triggers.
A card with no affiliation star. Can be played for its event by either side. The event triggers in the playing player's favor.
The number on a card (one to four) indicating how many operations it provides for influence placement, coups, or realignment.
The phase at the start of each turn where both players simultaneously reveal one card. Higher ops resolves first. Both events trigger.
One turn within the action phase where a player plays a single card. Six rounds in Early War turns, seven in Mid/Late War turns. USSR always goes first.
An operation spending ops to add influence markers. One op per marker in friendly or adjacent countries. Two ops per marker in opponent-controlled countries.
An operation targeting one country. Roll die plus ops value versus double stability. Success removes enemy influence and adds yours. Battleground coups lower DEFCON.
An operation targeting one country. Both sides roll with modifiers. Difference removes loser's influence. No influence added. No presence required.
Discarding a card to attempt advancement on the Space Race track. The event does not trigger. Card must meet minimum ops. Roll to advance. One attempt per turn.
The player whose action is currently being resolved. Critical for DEFCON: if DEFCON hits one during your action, you lose even if it was an opponent's event triggering on your card play.
Countries connected by lines on the map are adjacent. Influence placement requires adjacency or existing presence. Some countries are adjacent to superpowers directly.
As DEFCON drops, certain regions become restricted for coups and realignment. DEFCON 4 restricts Europe battlegrounds. DEFCON 3 adds Asia. DEFCON 2 adds the Middle East.
The highest-value region, split into Western and Eastern Europe. Control of Europe during scoring triggers an instant win. Worth the most VP at every scoring level.
A central region with key battlegrounds including Iran, Iraq, and Israel. Often the site of early game coups and influence battles.
A large region including Southeast Asia as a sub-region. Contains several battlegrounds. The China Card provides bonus ops when spent entirely here.
A southern region with many non-battleground countries. Becomes more important in the Mid War when its scoring card enters the deck.
A small region close to the USA. Contains battlegrounds like Cuba and Panama. Important for USA defense and USSR expansion.
A moderate-value region with battlegrounds including Brazil and Argentina. Often contested in the Mid War period.
An event card like Arab-Israeli War or Korean War that requires a modified die roll to succeed. Success grants influence, VP, and Military Operations.
An event card marked with an asterisk that is permanently removed from the game after its event triggers. Non-asterisk events go to the discard and will recirculate.
The result of DEFCON reaching one. The phasing player loses instantly. The most dramatic and feared outcome in the game.
